Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882年}}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994年}}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882年}}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994年}}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |image = [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun (front square on the left, back round on the right) |Alias = Notose Kofun{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} |Group = Okina Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}} Notose 598 (Aza Ohogi) (within the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} precincts) |Latitude degrees = 35|Latitude minutes = 19|Latitude seconds = 47.98 |Longitude degrees = 136|Longitude minutes = 19|Longitude seconds = 19.59 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} |Size = 46.2m mound length |Period = Mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}} |Burial Facility = {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (interior includes {{ill|Iegata-sekkan|ja|家形石棚}}) |Buried Individual = (Presumed) Chieftain of the {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} |Artifacts = Numerous grave goods, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|垣輪}}, and pottery shards |Historic Site = Shiga Prefectural Historic Site: Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |Cultural Property = Artifacts (Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property) |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Yamatsuteru Shrine
Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882年}}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994年}}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |image = [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun (front square on the left, back round on the right) |Alias = Notose Kofun{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} |Group = Okina Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}} Notose 598 (Aza Ohogi) (within the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} precincts) |Latitude degrees = 35|Latitude minutes = 19|Latitude seconds = 47.98 |Longitude degrees = 136|Longitude minutes = 19|Longitude seconds = 19.59 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} |Size = 46.2m mound length |Period = Mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}} |Burial Facility = {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (interior includes {{ill|Iegata-sekkan|ja|家形石棚}}) |Buried Individual = (Presumed) Chieftain of the {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} |Artifacts = Numerous grave goods, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|垣輪}}, and pottery shards |Historic Site = Shiga Prefectural Historic Site: Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |Cultural Property = Artifacts (Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property) |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Yamatsuteru Shrine
Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882年}}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994年}}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |image = [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun (front square on the left, back round on the right) |Alias = Notose Kofun{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} |Group = Okina Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}} Notose 598 (Aza Ohogi) (within the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} precincts) |Latitude degrees = 35|Latitude minutes = 19|Latitude seconds = 47.98 |Longitude degrees = 136|Longitude minutes = 19|Longitude seconds = 19.59 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} |Size = 46.2m mound length |Period = Mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}} |Burial Facility = {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (interior includes {{ill|Iegata-sekkan|ja|家形石棚}}) |Buried Individual = (Presumed) Chieftain of the {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} |Artifacts = Numerous grave goods, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|垣輪}}, and pottery shards |Historic Site = Shiga Prefectural Historic Site: Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |Cultural Property = Artifacts (Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property) |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Yamatsuteru Shrine
Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882年}}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994年}}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. File:山津照神社古墳出土 装身具類.JPG|Copper mirrors and magatama beads
{{small|Displayed at Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum.}} File:山津照神社古墳出土 馬具類.JPG|Horse trappings
{{small|Displayed at Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum.}} File:山津照神社古墳出土 須恵器.JPG|Sue pottery pottery
{{small|Displayed at Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum.}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}} - Houses and displays artifacts from the Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun. * Maibara City Okina Haniwa Museum - Features a reconstructed model of the Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == {{small|(Sources not directly cited in this article)}} * Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory. "Exploring the 6th Century Around Lake Biwa." revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |image = [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun (front square on the left, back round on the right) |Alias = Notose Kofun{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} |Group = Okina Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}} Notose 598 (Aza Ohogi) (within the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} precincts) |Latitude degrees = 35|Latitude minutes = 19|Latitude seconds = 47.98 |Longitude degrees = 136|Longitude minutes = 19|Longitude seconds = 19.59 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} |Size = 46.2m mound length |Period = Mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}} |Burial Facility = {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (interior includes {{ill|Iegata-sekkan|ja|家形石棚}}) |Buried Individual = (Presumed) Chieftain of the {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} |Artifacts = Numerous grave goods, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|垣輪}}, and pottery shards |Historic Site = Shiga Prefectural Historic Site: Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |Cultural Property = Artifacts (Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property) |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Yamatsuteru Shrine
Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882年}}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994年}}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. File:山津照神社古墳出土 装身具類.JPG|Copper mirrors and magatama beads
{{small|Displayed at Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum.}} File:山津照神社古墳出土 馬具類.JPG|Horse trappings
{{small|Displayed at Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum.}} File:山津照神社古墳出土 須恵器.JPG|Sue pottery pottery
{{small|Displayed at Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum.}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}} - Houses and displays artifacts from the Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun. * Maibara City Okina Haniwa Museum - Features a reconstructed model of the Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == {{small|(Sources not directly cited in this article)}} * Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory. "Exploring the 6th Century Around Lake Biwa." Kyoto University, 1995. == See Also! == {{Commonscat|Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun}} * {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} * {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Kofun (front square on the left, back round on the right) |Alias = Notose Kofun{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} |Group = Okina Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}} Notose 598 (Aza Ohogi) (within the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} precincts) |Latitude degrees = 35|Latitude minutes = 19|Latitude seconds = 47.98 |Longitude degrees = 136|Longitude minutes = 19|Longitude seconds = 19.59 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} |Size = 46.2m mound length |Period = Mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}} |Burial Facility = {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (interior includes {{ill|Iegata-sekkan|ja|家形石棚}}) |Buried Individual = (Presumed) Chieftain of the {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} |burial goods = Numerous grave goods, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|垣輪}}, and pottery shards |Historic Site = Shiga Prefectural Historic Site: Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |Cultural Property = Artifacts (Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property) |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Yamatsuteru Shrine
Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882年}}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994年}}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. Kofun (front square on the left, back round on the right) |Alias = Notose Kofun{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} |Group = Okina Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}} Notose 598 (Aza Ohogi) (within the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} precincts) |Latitude degrees = 35|Latitude minutes = 19|Latitude seconds = 47.98 |Longitude degrees = 136|Longitude minutes = 19|Longitude seconds = 19.59 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} |Size = 46.2m mound length |Period = Mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}} |Burial Facility = {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (interior includes {{ill|Iegata-sekkan|ja|家形石棚}}) |Buried Individual = (Presumed) Chieftain of the {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} |burial goods = Numerous grave goods, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|垣輪}}, and pottery shards |Historic Site = Shiga Prefectural Historic Site: Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |Cultural Property = Artifacts (Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property) |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Yamatsuteru Shrine
Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957年}}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969年}}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957th year }}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969th year }}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |Label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957th year }}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969th year }}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. "Exploring the 6th Century Around Lake Biwa." Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957th year }}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969th year }}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. revision.comment: attempt at mass correcting bad parameters on indofox ancient site

{{afc comment|1=now we have a 'Burial Facility' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |image = [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun (front square on the left, back round on the right) |Alias = Notose Kofun{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} |Group = Okina Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}} Notose 598 (Aza Ohogi) (within the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} precincts) |coordinatess = 35|coordinatess = 19|coordinatess = 47.98 |coordinatess = 136|coordinatess = 19|coordinatess = 19.59 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} |Size = 46.2m mound length |Period = Mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}} |Burial Facility = {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (interior includes {{ill|Iegata-sekkan|ja|家形石棚}}) |Buried Individual = (Presumed) Chieftain of the {{ill|Okina Clan|ja|恩長氏}} |burial goods = Numerous grave goods, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|垣輪}}, and pottery shards |Historic Site = Shiga Prefectural Historic Site: Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun |Cultural Property = Artifacts (Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property) |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Yamatsuteru Shrine
Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |map_dot_label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957th year }}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969th year }}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957th year }}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969th year }}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |map_dot_label Position = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957th year }}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969th year }}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969. Kofun |Icon = Zenpō-kōen-fun-West |map_dot_label = top }} '''Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun''' (山湾照神社古壇, ''Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun'') is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古壇}} (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Maibara City|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}. It features a {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後土壇}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) design and is part of the Okina Kofun Cluster. Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site, the artifacts excavated from the site are also recognized as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties. == Overview == [[File:Yamatsuteru-jinja Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Kofun (north side)}}{{small|Front square on the right, back round on the left.}]][[ja:山津照神社古墳]] The kofun is located at the southern edge of the Yokoyama Hills in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture (Hokubu Region). It is one of the representative late-period tumuli in the Hokubu Region{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. Together with other mounds such as Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun, Gobettō Kofun, Hitotsukayama Kofun, and Kitsunezuka Kofun, it forms the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Presently, it is situated within the precincts of the {{ill|Yamatsuteru Shrine|ja|山湾照神社}} and was discovered during shrine construction work in 1882 (Meiji 15). At that time, a stone coffin and artifacts were unearthed. Parts of the mound have been leveled over time, and in 1994 (Heisei 6), an investigation by Kyoto University was conducted. The mound has a developed keyhole-shaped design typical of the late tumulus period, with the front square facing west. It measures 46.2 meters in length. Although no terraced construction or stone covering has been identified, "Iwami-style" shield-shaped haniwa and cylindrical haniwa have been arranged. Ritual sites in the northern constricted area suggest the possible existence of a {{ill|Tsukuri-dashi|ja|造出|1=Platform-style projection}} structure{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. No surrounding moat has been found{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. The burial facility features a yokoana-style stone chamber containing an iegata-sekkan. Excavations in the Meiji period revealed numerous grave goods, including gilt-bronze crowns, swords, and crystal magatama beads. The mound is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-6th century during the late Kofun period{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. It is considered the successor to the Tsuka-no-Koshi Kofun as the chieftain’s tomb within the Okina Kofun Cluster{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}} and the last keyhole-shaped tumulus in the {{ill|Sakata District|ja|坂田郡}} area{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. The cluster is believed to be the burial site for chieftains of the Okina Clan, with this kofun being one of their key tombs{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76-97}}. Historically, it was even speculated to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Okina|ja|恩長宿禦王}}, father of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. == Archaeological Timeline == * {{ill|1882|ja|1882nd year }}: Discovered during shrine construction, uncovering stone coffins and artifacts{{Sfn|Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }}: Excavation of artifacts conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1957|ja|1957th year }}: Majority of excavated items designated as Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties (August 26). * {{ill|1969|ja|1969th year }}: Designated as a Shiga Prefectural Historic Site (September 12). * {{ill|1994|ja|1994th year }}: Survey and excavation of the mound conducted by Kyoto University Archaeology Laboratory{{Sfn|Investigation of Okina Town Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun|1995}}. * {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}: Artifacts not previously designated were recognized as cultural properties by Okina Town (later Maibara City). * {{ill|2006|ja|2006年}}: Additional artifacts were included in the Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties listing (March 17). == Cultural Properties == === Shiga Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties: ** Artifacts excavated from Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun *** Items include gilt-bronze crowns, iron swords, ceremonial pottery, and decorative fittings. Since 1961, most artifacts have been housed at the {{ill|Lake Biwa Museum|ja|湖濱博物館}}, with some later transferred to the {{ill|Azuchi Castle Archaeology Museum|ja|安園城考古博物館}}. *** First designation: August 26, 1957; additional designation: March 17, 2006. * Historic Site: ** Yamatsuteru Shrine Kofun - Designated September 12, 1969.
